title: Why Your Shorts Get Views But Your Long-Form Gets Ignored

transcript.text: Look at these two channels. They have nearly identical shorts views, but only one of these channels is actually turning that attention into long-form views, while the other can barely crack 10,000 views on their longest videos. Why is that? You know I'm about to say this, but it's not luck, it's not the niche, it's not the algorithm playing favorites. There's one specific thing that these fast-growing channels are doing that the channels who are stuck are not, and it simply comes down to cohesion. What I mean is their shorts and their long-form videos look like they came from the same channel. They look the same, they sound the same, and the thing the viewer sees in the first 30 seconds after clicking on the long-form video is exactly what they just saw when they watched that short a little while ago. Meanwhile, the channels who are stuck don't have this cohesion. Their shorts and their long-form videos look like two different products that live under the same banner. Let's look at Sarah's channel from earlier with a whopping 1.4 million subscribers. Her shorts pull in millions of views without breaking a sweat. Meanwhile, her long-forms barely average around 10,000 views, which is 100 times less than the views she's getting on her shorts. Her shorts are quick cuts, on-the-go, one-moment challenges. Stuff that you kind of watch, you get in, you get out. Meanwhile, the long-form videos are more like sit-down storytelling vlog style videos. And that's a completely different format. That's two different types of content coming from the same creator on the same channel. This is why it feels like even though you're doing everything you think you're supposed to be doing, like adding links to related videos, pinned comments, and calls to action, it's just still not working. Tools that let you pin a comment or link a related video do work, but only under the right conditions. So, let's talk about what those conditions actually are. This cohesion shows up in three different forms, and Wolfie nails all three of them. Let me show you what each one looks like, starting with the channel that has all three of them working in harmony together right now with every upload. I introduce Cook Show with Trevor. You may have heard me bring this one up before. This is one of my favorite channels on YouTube. Here's one of his shorts, and let's look at the visual identity here. An apron, a brown background, deadpan delivery, and pizza. Now, let's look at one of his long-form thumbnails. An apron, a brown background, a deadpan expression, and you guessed it, pizza. You're not thinking to yourself, "Oh, is that a short or a long-form video?" If you watch one or the other, your brain just recognizes this identity, this branding that Trevor's created right away. I love this example because this is three different types of cohesion just stacked on top of each other. But, let me break down each one on their own. The first form is voice and personality cohesion. This is the way you sound, the way you talk, the energy you bring on camera. It should be the same in both formats. So, for this we'll take a look at Bugs. The short is Bugs, and the long-form is still Bugs. He has the same cadence and the same persona in all the videos. >> Listen, I'm a weak little American. I get homesick. What am I supposed to do without my Big Macs and french fries? >> This all matters because of what's happening inside your viewer's head. When they get pulled into your short, they get pulled in because of you. It's your energy, it's your voice. If they tap through and then they meet a completely different person in the long-form videos, for example, it's Bugs, but you're getting the buttoned-up podcast version of this creator, your brain isn't going to register that this is a stranger. If your tone is mismatched, then your viewers are going to click away. The mistake so many creators are making is that they feel as though their long-form persona, for some reason, needs to be different than their short-form persona. But, in most cases, it shouldn't be this way. If you have a big personality or you're really reserved when you make videos, if someone's seeing you in a short that way, they should see you the same way when they click into your long-form content. It's that simple. The second form we're going to look at is world and universe cohesion. This is the visual world that your videos live in. It can be your set, it can be your framing, it can be recurring elements that you have appearing on screen, it can even be your color palette. Murphy's Diesel is a textbook case. It takes place in the same shop or sometimes outdoors with the same trucks, and he even uses the same direct-to-camera filming angles. If you see a thumbnail from his channel, you know you don't have to read the channel name. The setting itself tells you whose video it is. And of course, the big fabulous beard probably doesn't hurt. But, it doesn't have to be some big complicated setup, either. Heather Cox Richardson does it without changing anything inside her home. She's in the same room with the same framing, with the same simple background, and it works. Every video feels like walking back into the same space you were in last time. Your setting's cohesion is especially important because visual recognition is faster than someone's brain can consciously process. A viewer could be scrolling the YouTube homepage, and if your thumbnail pops up, that visual familiarity would register in milliseconds. So, it's a pretty big missed opportunity if your long-form video thumbnails have not the same visual world and aesthetic that your shorts would typically have. The third form is subject and authority cohesion, or said another way, the thing your channel is about. If you're kind of new to understanding the YouTube algorithm, what you may not know is that YouTube isn't recommending your shorts or your long-form based on who you are. It's recommending your videos based on what they're about. Remember, the job of the YouTube algorithm is to keep a viewers on the platform for as long as possible, just individual people with their own interests. So, YouTube is going to find videos that cater to those interests, and it tries to show that viewer as many of those videos as possible. So, if you have a short about drawing and a long-form video about something completely different, YouTube has no incentive to give the drawing audience your videos about whatever else you're covering on your channel. However, this is just one that most creators are getting wrong, including myself. I used to be this way. You offer one thing in your shorts, and you offer a completely different format in your long-form videos. Today I learned science isn't a science creator in a broad sense. They're a more specific kind. It's science as detective work. Every video takes one intriguing mystery and walks you through what the evidence actually shows. The shorts compress a two a case in 60 seconds, and the long-form videos take one mystery and they go deep. What's important here is that the format stretches, but the lens never changes. It's always, "Here's a puzzle. Here's what science reveals." Channels like this aren't defined by their topic, but rather their angle. Today I learned covers everything from plane crashes to deep sea biology, but the promise never changes. Here's a mystery. Here's what the evidence shows. That consistent angle is what makes her an authority, not the subject matter. And if you split that angle between formats, for example, the fun take lives in your shorts and the serious take is in your long-form content, then you're not an authority on anything. Now you're just a person making random videos and people don't want to subscribe to that. What they want to subscribe to is somebody with a reliable angle and who's an authority on the topic that they cover. Okay, so what is the actual tangible thing you can do to fix this on your channel? First, what you want to do is run a litmus test on what we talked about earlier. you to take your last five shorts and put them alongside your last five long-form videos. Then ask yourself, would a stranger look at these two videos and assume they're from the same channel? And if the answer is no, then one of the forms we just covered is broken, potentially multiple forms we just covered. Or if you feel like you need an extra set of eyes, a third-party opinion on this question, then you can always prompt our AI coach. The answer you get could be a good jumping off point so your content can become more aligned. The second thing, if you're trying to have long-form and short-form crossover, then your long-form thumbnails need to look like they came from the same person who made that short I just watched. They shouldn't necessarily be identical, but they should be recognizable. And again, this can be in a number of ways. They could have the same color palette, they could have the same facial expression, or they could be on the same backdrop that you usually film your shorts on. The goal is to give off the same energy. If somebody watches your short, taps into your profile, and looks at your long-form content, then the thumbnails they're seeing should visually echo what they just saw back in the short. If your shorts are punchy and chaotic, but your long-form thumbnails look really buttoned up and professional, then there's a disconnect and that handoff is not going to work from short viewer to long-form viewer. The third thing, and something to not underestimate, would be the first few seconds of your long-form video. Even in that first 5 seconds of time, viewers are already deciding whether or not they want to stick around and watch the rest of your video. Some people have more patience, but ultimately, we're all looking for a reason to move on to the next video. Unfortunately, so many creators miss this part completely. They spend 20 hours making this perfect short with quick punchy edits and a lot of chaos and fun, and then they make these long-form videos where the first thing people see when they click on them is, "Hey, welcome back to my channel. I'm Dan." So on and so forth. But that same viewer just potentially sat through a short with all this energy and charisma, and now they're being told, "Whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa, whoa, settle down. You need to sit through this podcast intro while I tell you to like the video." So the fix is simple. If your short-form has really high energy, then your long-form intros at the very least your intros should also have that same energy. If there's got to be a slow down later on, then let that slow down happen naturally after you've earned that viewer's trust. One of the most important things to consider when you're making short videos and longer videos is this. Viewers who come to shorts versus viewers watching long-form videos are in two completely different mindsets. The shorts feed is inherently passive. Viewers are just kind of scrolling, YouTube is serving up all kinds of stuff to them, and they're just looking for some quick hits. Maybe they're just killing some time. The long-form mindset is totally different. A viewer has to see a video and then decide for themselves whether or not they're going to engage with it. If they click, they're willing to give you 8 minutes, 12 minutes, 20 minutes, and that is just a vastly different behavior from a shorts viewer. And frustratingly, a shorts viewer is completely capable of also being a long-form viewer, able to sit down and give you 20 minutes of their time. And here's what this actually means for you as a creator. For one, you can't just chop a long-form into a short and expect it to work. And you also can't take a short and simply expand on it to make it a long-form video. Think of it like junk food versus a real meal. Shorts are simply a snack, whereas the long-form videos are the meal. Both are great, and they can even be cohesive with each other, but people eat one or the other because they're simply hungry for different things at different times. And don't forget to actually use the tools that YouTube has provided for us. For example, on every single short, you can actually click a button to add a link to a related long-form video. YouTube has spent years building this pipeline for for handoff between shorts and long form. Use shorts for quick wins. One specific takeaway, one demonstration, one reaction. Use long form for depth and series. This could be telling a story, building an argument, or walking through a process. When you pick the right format for the right kind of content, your channel reads as intentional. But when you mismatch them, your channel is going to read as random and confusing. From there, cohesion breaks, even if your world, voice, and subject are all in the same place. The big shift I want you to take away is this. Stop worrying about this bridge between your shorts and your longs, and instead focus on whether your channel looks like it is one channel. Once your voice, world, and subject all line up, then the bridge isn't something that you have to manually build anymore. Now, that can engineer itself, and all you have to do is keep making content. At the start of this video, I showed you two channels that were pulling in basically the same amount of views for shorts, but their long form strategies couldn't have been more different. And all that was was a matter of one channel having that cohesion and one not. Earlier I said that the algorithm routes viewers to your content based on what your video's about and not who you are. So, what ends up happening is that people end up building several audiences instead of one audience. And that's why views will look like they spike sometimes and fall off other times. So, that's the thing standing between you and steady growth. And that's what this video breaks down right over here.
